glossary_terms.definition: Know Your Customer (KYC) refers to the set of processes and controls that financial institutions and regulated entities use to verify the identity of their customers, assess their risk profiles, and comply with anti-money laundering (AML) and counter-terrorist financing (CTF) regulations.

Enhanced KYC, often referred to as Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD), is a deeper level of customer due diligence applied to higher-risk customers, products, or transactions, typically involving additional identity checks, source-of-funds verification, and ongoing monitoring.

KYC remediation is the process of updating, correcting, or completing existing Know Your Customer records to ensure they meet current regulatory standards and internal policy requirements, often triggered by regulatory reviews or internal audits.

The KYC refresh cycle is the defined schedule on which customer identity and risk information must be reviewed and updated, typically based on the risk rating of the customer (e.g., annually for high-risk, every 3–5 years for low-risk).

Anti-Money Laundering (AML) refers to laws, regulations, and procedures designed to prevent criminals from disguising illegally obtained funds as legitimate income, including requirements for customer due diligence, transaction monitoring, and suspicious activity reporting.

Beneficial ownership refers to the natural person or persons who ultimately own or control a customer or on whose behalf a transaction is being conducted, regardless of whose name the legal ownership is in.

Customer Due Diligence (CDD) is the process of collecting and assessing information about a customer to verify their identity, understand the nature of the relationship, and evaluate the risk they may pose in terms of money laundering or terrorist financing.

A Politically Exposed Person (PEP) is an individual who holds or has held a prominent public function, as well as their close associates and family members, and who may pose a higher risk of involvement in bribery or corruption.

Open banking is a regulatory and technology-driven approach that allows customers to securely share their financial data with third-party providers through standardized APIs, enabling new services such as account aggregation, payment initiation, and personalized financial products.

The Second Payment Services Directive (PSD2) is an EU directive that regulates payment services and payment service providers throughout the European Union and European Economic Area, mandating strong customer authentication and access-to-account rules that underpin open banking.

Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) is a European regulatory requirement under PSD2 that mandates multi-factor authentication for certain electronic payments, typically requiring two or more elements such as knowledge, possession, and inherence.

Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) is a consumer credit model that allows customers to purchase goods or services immediately and pay for them over time, often interest-free, typically offered at the point of sale by fintechs or merchants.

A crypto-asset is a digital representation of value or rights that can be transferred and stored electronically using distributed ledger technology or similar, including cryptocurrencies, utility tokens, and some stablecoins.

A stablecoin is a type of crypto-asset designed to maintain a stable value relative to a reference asset, such as a fiat currency or a basket of assets, often backed by reserves or managed via algorithms.

A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is a structured process required under GDPR for high-risk personal data processing activities, used to identify, assess, and mitigate privacy risks to individuals.

The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is the European Union’s primary data protection law, setting rules for how organizations collect, use, and protect personal data and providing individuals with rights over their data.

An open banking API is a standardized application programming interface that allows third-party providers to securely access bank account data or initiate payments on behalf of customers, subject to regulatory and consent requirements.

A regulatory sandbox is a framework set up by a financial regulator that allows fintech firms to test innovative products, services, or business models in a controlled environment under relaxed regulatory requirements.

Tokenization is the process of converting rights to an asset into a digital token on a distributed ledger or blockchain, enabling fractional ownership, programmable transfers, or improved settlement efficiency.

Transaction monitoring is the ongoing process of reviewing customer transactions in real time or near-real time to detect unusual or suspicious activity that may indicate money laundering, fraud, or sanctions violations.

segments.name: Startups
segments.description: Early-stage and high-growth companies looking for practical legal and fintech guidance, including fundraising, compliance basics, and product launch checklists.

segments.name: Enterprises
segments.description: Large and mid-size organizations focusing on scalable compliance programs, global regulatory change management, and complex fintech partnerships.

segments.name: Financial institutions
segments.description: Banks, payment institutions, e-money firms, and credit providers navigating evolving regulations such as open banking, AML, and crypto rules.

segments.name: Regulators & policy teams
segments.description: Supervisory authorities, central banks, and policy teams seeking comparative insights on fintech regulation, supervision, and market developments.

segment_subcategories.name: Legal basics
segment_subcategories.description: Foundational legal topics for startups, such as incorporation, founder agreements, data protection basics, and early-stage compliance checklists.

segment_subcategories.name: Fundraising
segment_subcategories.description: Guides and checklists on SAFEs, convertible notes, equity rounds, and investor due diligence from a legal and regulatory perspective.

segment_subcategories.name: Compliance checklists
segment_subcategories.description: Short, actionable compliance checklists tailored to early-stage fintech and SaaS startups, including KYC, AML, and data privacy basics.

segment_subcategories.name: Risk & compliance programs
segment_subcategories.description: Content on building and maintaining enterprise-wide risk and compliance frameworks, including policies, controls, and regulatory reporting.

segment_subcategories.name: Payments & open banking
segment_subcategories.description: Deep dives into open banking, instant payments, BNPL, and other payment innovations from a legal and regulatory standpoint.

segment_subcategories.name: Regulatory updates
segment_subcategories.description: Summaries and analysis of new and proposed regulations, supervisory guidance, and cross-border coordination in fintech and financial services.

regulation_entries.title: US AML Reform: BSA Modernization and Beneficial Ownership Implementation
regulation_entries.description: Implements key elements of the Anti-Money Laundering Act and modernizes the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA), including beneficial ownership reporting requirements and risk-based supervision expectations.

regulation_entries.title: US CDD Rule Update: Beneficial Ownership and Risk Rating Enhancements
regulation_entries.description: Refines customer due diligence obligations, expanding beneficial ownership data collection and clarifying expectations for ongoing KYC refresh cycles.

regulation_entries.title: US AML Guidance on Virtual Asset Service Providers
regulation_entries.description: Clarifies how existing AML obligations, including KYC and travel rule requirements, apply to virtual asset service providers and certain DeFi arrangements.

regulation_entries.title: EU AML Package: Extended Travel Rule for Crypto-Asset Transfers
regulation_entries.description: Extends the funds transfer travel rule to crypto-asset transfers, requiring collection and transmission of originator and beneficiary information for certain transactions.

regulation_entries.title: EU MiCA: Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation Final Text
regulation_entries.description: Establishes the first EU-wide licensing and conduct framework for issuers of asset-referenced tokens and e-money tokens, and for crypto-asset service providers.

regulation_entries.title: UK Open Banking Roadmap: Phase 2 Customer Protection Measures
regulation_entries.description: Introduces enhanced dispute resolution, stronger customer authentication requirements, and minimum service levels for open banking APIs.

regulation_entries.title: EU PSD3 and Payment Services Regulation Proposal
regulation_entries.description: Proposes PSD3 and a new Payment Services Regulation to replace parts of PSD2, updating open banking access rules, SCA exemptions, and incident reporting.

regulation_entries.title: US Supervisory Guidance on Buy Now, Pay Later Products
regulation_entries.description: Outlines expectations for BNPL underwriting, disclosures, dispute handling, and credit reporting, aligning BNPL practices with existing consumer credit rules.

regulation_entries.title: UK Crypto Tax: HMRC Manual Update on DeFi and Staking
regulation_entries.description: Updates HMRC guidance on the tax treatment of DeFi lending, staking, and liquidity pools, including capital versus revenue distinctions.

regulation_entries.title: EU GDPR: Updated EDPB Guidelines on Data Protection Impact Assessments
regulation_entries.description: Clarifies when DPIAs are mandatory, provides new high-risk processing examples for fintechs, and updates guidance on AI and profiling.

regulation_entries.title: FATF Guidance Update on the Travel Rule for Virtual Assets
regulation_entries.description: Updates global standards on how virtual asset service providers should implement the travel rule, including de minimis thresholds and unhosted wallets.

regulation_entries.title: US AML and Sanctions Screening Expectations for AI-Based Tools
regulation_entries.description: Provides supervisory expectations for the use of AI and machine learning in sanctions and transaction screening, emphasizing explainability and model governance.

regulation_entries.title: UK Pro-Innovation Approach to AI Regulation White Paper
regulation_entries.description: Sets out the UK governments high-level principles for regulating AI, including expectations for financial services supervisors and regulated firms.

regulation_entries.title: EU AI Act: Provisional Political Agreement
regulation_entries.description: Reaches a provisional agreement on the EU AI Act, introducing risk-based requirements that will affect AI systems used in credit scoring, AML monitoring, and fraud detection.

regulation_entries.title: APAC Regional Regulatory Sandbox for Crypto and Tokenization
regulation_entries.description: Launches a cross-border regulatory sandbox for tokenization and crypto-asset pilots, with simplified onboarding and tailored reporting requirements.

regulation_entries.title: US Crypto Tax: Broker Reporting Rules Under Infrastructure Act
regulation_entries.description: Implements new information reporting obligations for digital asset brokers, impacting exchanges, payment processors, and some wallet providers.

articles.title: US AML Reform 2023: BSA Modernization Explained
articles.summary: Walk through the key elements of the 2023 US AML reform package, including BSA modernization, beneficial ownership reporting, and risk-based supervision.
articles.body: This explainer breaks down the core components of the 2023 US AML reform agenda, focusing on how BSA modernization, beneficial ownership reporting, and risk-based supervision requirements affect banks and fintechs. It outlines which entities are in scope, what data must be collected, and how regulators expect institutions to update their AML programs. Practical checklists help compliance teams prioritize implementation workstreams.

articles.title: US CDD Rule Changes: Beneficial Ownership and Risk Ratings
articles.summary: How the updated US customer due diligence rule changes beneficial ownership collection and KYC refresh expectations.
articles.body: The updated CDD rule refines how institutions identify and verify beneficial owners, with clearer expectations around risk-based thresholds and ongoing monitoring. This guide explains the regulatory text in plain language, maps new requirements to existing KYC processes, and provides sample risk-rating criteria and refresh cycles. It also highlights practical pitfalls identified in recent enforcement actions.

articles.title: How US AML Rules Apply to Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs)
articles.summary: A VASP-focused walkthrough of how US AML and travel rule obligations apply to exchanges, custodians, and some DeFi arrangements.
articles.body: Virtual asset service providers face many of the same AML obligations as traditional financial institutions, but implementation details differ. This explainer maps key US AML requirements to typical VASP activities, including onboarding, transaction monitoring, and application of the travel rule. It also discusses grey areas for DeFi protocols and practical approaches for risk-based controls.

articles.title: EU Crypto Travel Rule: What CASPs Need to Implement by 2024
articles.summary: An implementation-focused explainer of the EU’s extended travel rule for crypto-asset transfers.
articles.body: The EU’s extension of the travel rule to crypto-asset transfers requires crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) to collect, verify, and transmit originator and beneficiary information. This article explains which transactions are in scope, how thresholds work, and how to manage transfers involving unhosted wallets. It also outlines data model considerations and coordination with banking partners.

articles.title: EU MiCA Overview: Licensing, Stablecoins, and CASP Obligations
articles.summary: A structured overview of the Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) and what it means for issuers and CASPs.
articles.body: MiCA is the EU’s flagship framework for crypto-assets, introducing authorization requirements for issuers of asset-referenced tokens and e-money tokens, and a harmonized licensing regime for crypto-asset service providers. This article breaks MiCA down into modules—authorization, conduct of business, governance, and market abuse—and highlights which entities should start preparing now. Practical timelines and a role-based checklist are included for legal and product teams.

articles.title: UK Open Banking Roadmap Phase 2: Customer Protection in Practice
articles.summary: A beginner-friendly guide to the UK’s Phase 2 open banking roadmap, focusing on customer protection, complaints, and SCA.
articles.body: Phase 2 of the UK open banking roadmap introduces new minimum service levels, clearer complaints handling expectations, and enhancements to strong customer authentication journeys. This guide, aimed at product and compliance teams, explains what must change in user flows, API behaviour, and customer communications. It includes simple diagrams of consent journeys and a checklist for prioritizing fixes.

articles.title: PSD3 and the New EU Payment Services Regulation: A Fintech Explainer
articles.summary: What fintechs and banks need to know about the proposed PSD3 and EU Payment Services Regulation.
articles.body: The European Commission’s PSD3 and Payment Services Regulation proposals aim to update PSD2, strengthen consumer protection, and refine open banking access rules. This explainer summarises the key changes, from payment institution licensing to SCA exemptions and incident reporting. It also discusses likely implementation timelines and how firms can future-proof their API and consent architectures.

articles.title: US BNPL Supervisory Guidance: Key Takeaways for Providers
articles.summary: A summary of US supervisory expectations for Buy Now, Pay Later providers, covering underwriting, disclosures, and credit reporting.
articles.body: Regulators are converging on the view that BNPL products should follow many of the same rules as traditional consumer credit. This article summarises US supervisory guidance on underwriting standards, marketing and disclosures, dispute handling, and credit reporting. It also includes a practical compliance checklist and suggests governance steps for boards overseeing BNPL portfolios.

articles.title: UK Crypto Tax and DeFi: HMRC’s 2023 Manual Update Explained
articles.summary: Beginner-friendly guidance on how HMRC’s 2023 update treats DeFi lending, staking, and liquidity pools.
articles.body: HMRC’s latest manual update clarifies when DeFi returns are taxed as capital versus income, how to treat token swaps, and what records taxpayers should keep. This guide walks through common DeFi scenarios with simple diagrams and examples. It is written for practitioners and founders who need to answer basic customer questions and design tax-aware product features.

articles.title: Updated GDPR DPIA Guidelines: What Fintechs Should Focus On
articles.summary: A fintech-focused breakdown of the EDPB’s updated DPIA guidance, with examples from payments and lending.
articles.body: The EDPB’s revised DPIA guidelines include new examples of high-risk processing relevant to fintechs, such as behavioural profiling and automated credit decisions. This article explains when a DPIA is mandatory, how to scope it effectively, and how to document risk mitigations. It also touches on intersections with AI use cases and cross-border data transfers.

articles.title: FATF Travel Rule Guidance for Virtual Assets: Global Overview
articles.summary: How FATF’s travel rule guidance for virtual assets affects VASPs, banks, and crypto startups globally.
articles.body: FATF’s updated travel rule guidance sets expectations for how VASPs should identify originators and beneficiaries, handle unhosted wallets, and coordinate cross-border transfers. This overview explains the core concepts and highlights where national implementations diverge. It includes a comparison table for major jurisdictions and outlines practical steps to build a global compliance roadmap.

articles.title: US Expectations for AI-based Sanctions and AML Screening
articles.summary: What supervisors expect when financial institutions deploy AI in sanctions and transaction screening.
articles.body: Regulators increasingly scrutinise how AI models are deployed in sanctions and AML screening. This article outlines supervisory expectations around explainability, model validation, data quality, and governance. It also discusses how to align AI tooling with existing model risk frameworks and what documentation examiners are likely to request.

articles.title: UK AI Regulation White Paper: Implications for Financial Services
articles.summary: An overview of the UK’s pro-innovation AI white paper and what it means for banks and fintechs.
articles.body: The UK’s AI regulation white paper proposes a principles-based, sector-led approach. This article summarises those principles and explores how financial regulators are likely to interpret them in relation to credit scoring, fraud detection, and AML monitoring. It also compares the UK’s direction with the EU AI Act and provides questions risk teams should ask when approving AI use cases.

articles.title: EU AI Act Provisional Agreement: Key Points for Compliance Teams
articles.summary: A compliance-focused overview of the EU AI Act’s provisional agreement and its risk-based framework.
articles.body: The provisional agreement on the EU AI Act sets out a tiered risk model, with stricter obligations for high-risk systems, including many used in financial services. This overview explains classification criteria, obligations for high-risk systems, and prohibited practices. It also highlights governance, documentation, and conformity assessment requirements relevant to compliance and product teams.

articles.title: APAC Regulatory Sandbox: Testing Crypto and Tokenization Use Cases
articles.summary: How APAC’s cross-border regulatory sandbox supports tokenization pilots and crypto-asset experiments.
articles.body: Several APAC regulators have launched a shared sandbox to enable cross-border tokenization pilots. This article explains eligibility criteria, how the sandbox interacts with existing licensing regimes, and what supervisory oversight participants can expect. It also discusses practical lessons from early cohorts and how global compliance teams can leverage sandbox learnings.

articles.title: US Crypto Tax: New Broker Reporting Rules Explained
articles.summary: A guide to upcoming US information reporting obligations for digital asset brokers.
articles.body: Under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, many digital asset intermediaries will become subject to expanded information reporting rules. This guide explains who is in scope, what forms must be filed, and how to interpret the definition of a ‘broker’ in the crypto context. It also covers practical data challenges and how exchanges and platforms can prepare.

articles.title: EU Crypto Regulation Basics for Startups: 10-Point Launch Checklist (2024)
articles.summary: A concise, EU-focused checklist for crypto startups covering MiCA, AML, and data protection essentials before launch.
articles.body: This checklist is designed for early-stage EU crypto startups preparing to launch. It walks through 10 essential checks, from determining whether your token is in scope of MiCA, to registering as a CASP, documenting your AML framework, and mapping data flows for GDPR compliance. Each point includes references to primary legislation and questions to ask external counsel.

articles.title: EU Crypto Compliance in Under 10 Minutes: Ongoing Duties Checklist (2024)
articles.summary: An EU-focused ongoing compliance checklist for crypto startups, including reporting, KYC refresh, and incident handling.
articles.body: Once live, EU crypto startups must manage ongoing obligations across MiCA, AML rules, and data privacy. This checklist groups recurring duties into monthly, quarterly, and annual tasks. It covers KYC refresh cycles, suspicious transaction reporting, complaints handling, ICT incident reporting, and key governance updates founders should schedule into their calendars.

articles.title: Open Banking in the UK: Beginner’s Guide to Account Access APIs
articles.summary: A simple introduction to UK open banking account access, aimed at product and compliance beginners.
articles.body: This guide explains how UK open banking account access works, from the role of AISP permissions to consent flows and data minimisation. It avoids jargon and includes annotated screenshots of a typical consent journey. The final section provides a short checklist of questions for product managers and legal teams before shipping an open banking-powered feature.

articles.title: Open Banking Payments in the UK: Beginner’s Guide for Product Teams
articles.summary: A practical guide to UK open banking payment initiation for beginner-level product and legal teams.
articles.body: Payment initiation is one of the most powerful open banking capabilities. This guide explains how PISPs work, what strong customer authentication means in practice, and how to design user flows that meet regulatory expectations while remaining intuitive. It includes example risk checks, common error codes, and tips for collaborating with partner banks.

comments.body: Really helpful summary. The comparison table on underwriting expectations versus traditional instalment credit is exactly what I needed for our board memo.
Could you clarify whether the guidance expects BNPL providers to pull full credit reports for all customers, or only for higher-ticket transactions?
We used the checklist at the end to run a quick gap assessment. It mapped surprisingly well to our existing credit card framework.
Nice breakdown of disclosure expectations. A short example of compliant marketing copy would be a great addition.
We operate a white-labelled BNPL product. Do you read the guidance as applying equally to merchants that just embed our flow?
Thank you for calling out the credit reporting point. Thats been missing from a lot of other summaries.
Were updating our policies now. The governance section here helped us frame the board responsibilities more clearly.
One nuance worth adding: state law interplay on late fees can be even tougher than what the federal guidance implies.
Do you expect small-ticket pay in 4 products to move towards full Reg Z style disclosures based on this?
The questions for the board section was particularly useful. Shared it directly with our audit committee.
Would love to see a follow-up piece on how this interacts with buy now, pay later products offered at physical point of sale.
Great overview. The examples of risky marketing claims to avoid were very concrete.
We operate in multiple countries. This helped us separate the uniquely US issues from global BNPL themes.
The checklist format made this much easier to turn into a project plan for our compliance team.
Thank you for linking to the underlying guidance and not just secondary sources. Very useful for our internal training.
We just finished an internal audit themed around BNPL. This article aligned closely with what our auditors focused on.
Perfect level of detail for an early-stage team. We used this checklist in our first legal/compliance planning session.
The ongoing duties breakdown by month/quarter/year is super practical. Makes it easier to budget for compliance support.
Thanks for keeping this genuinely beginner-friendly. Shared with non-technical colleagues and it finally clicked for them.
Helpful overview of PISPs and SCA. The example error codes list saved us a lot of trial and error in integration.
